sourceDesc.p: Downloaded from https://medium.com/@louBurnard/a-curious-coincidence-d3f6b7c395d5 and translated from HTML to TEI

body.head: A curious coincidence
body.p: So it’s November 1970, and I’m still living in a cottage in Stanton St John where I am now into the second year of my BPhil, and therefore still getting paid by the state to read lots of 19th century novels, which is nice. I have had a bucolic summer doing just that, in the intervals of shifting bales of straw and looking after cows at Manor Farm, and now it’s time to start doing some serious work on that little old thesis thingie. But wait! Here’s a letter from my old friend Lola Pryce, who is busy in London learning to be an architect at some prestigious institution called the AA.

figure.head: “We have taken Oxford as our brief, as, you may know, it is having the first 1906A computer in the country, plus a whole computer research & operation faculty…”

body.p: Over the page, she elaborates further on her project:

figure.head: “… Computers are such charming things themselves; they need, of course, fantastic servicing — enormous air-conditioning ducts, dust filters, totally removable false floors capable of standing enormous point loads etc. etc. And then one considers such things as how to make a totally artificial environment pleasant; how to make life more bearable for those poor bitches, punch-card operators; do the engineers share a rest room with the research computer operators? (i.e. do the Proles actually share with the Intellectuals — I mean, it’s a real decision, my god)

body.p: All very interesting, and good to know that the British class system was alive and well even in this supposedly futuristic context. In 1970, I knew nothing of the University’s data processing, and I lost touch with Lola when I left Oxford for Malawi in 1972. And yet, when I returned to Oxford a couple of years later in April 1974 and got a proper job as a computer operator, my first few weeks were spent in the 1906A machine room designed by Lola and her cohorts, which still (I think) stands behind number 13 Banbury Road, though since converted to other uses. Coincidence? Or what?
